whatToFeed: Acidifying organic fertilizer such as ammonium sulfate, cottonseed meal, or a specialized rhododendron/azalea berry food in early spring.
pruningTips: Do not prune for the first 2-3 years. On established bushes, prune in late winter to remove dead, diseased, or low-crossing branches, and cut back older, woody canes to the ground to stimulate productive new wood.
flavorProfile: Sweet, tart, and deeply fruity, bursting with rich, refreshing juice.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant bare-root or container-grown bushes in early spring (mid-to-late April) as soon as the soil can be worked.
whenToHarvest: Harvest in mid-to-late summer when berries turn a uniform deep blue and pull away from the stem with a gentle touch.
usesForLargeHarvests: Freezing whole on baking sheets, baking into tarts or muffins, cooking down into preserves, blending into fresh fruit smoothies.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial shrub. Requires winter chilling hours to set fruit successfully the following spring.
plantingInstructions: Dig a hole twice as wide as the root ball and blend the native backfill soil with 50% acidic peat moss or aged pine bark. Plant the bush at the same depth as its nursery container, pack firmly, and cover with a thick 3-inch layer of pine needle or oak leaf mulch to protect the shallow root network.
growthRequirements: Demands moist, light, exceptionally well-draining, and highly acidic soil with a strict pH range between 4.5 and 4.8. Shallow, sensitive roots will rot in standing water but die off in dry periods, requiring steady, uniform hydration. Thrives in full sun.

whatToFeed: Balanced organic fruit fertilizer or bone meal in early spring and again immediately after the June harvest concludes.
pruningTips: Clip off early runners during the first year to force energy into building a massive main crown. Clip away old, diseased leaves post-harvest to allow fresh green expansion.
flavorProfile: Intensely sweet, juicy, and classic strawberry flavor with a firm, rich texture.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant bare-root crowns or starts outdoors in mid-to-late April as soon as the soil is workable.
whenToHarvest: Harvest daily in June when berries turn completely deep red from tip to stem and feel slightly soft.
usesForLargeHarvests: Baking into tarts, preserving into sweet jams, freezing whole for smoothies, macerating for cocktails.
hardinessNote: Hardy perennial ground fruit; benefits from a straw mulch cover in late November to protect shallow fruit buds over winter.
plantingInstructions: Dig a small shallow hole and build a tiny center dirt mound. Place the crown directly on top of the mound, spreading roots downward. Backfill so the soil line hits exactly at the midpoint of the central green crown; burying the crown entirely triggers rot.
growthRequirements: Requires rich, deeply tilled, well-aerated sandy loam with excellent drainage and a slightly acidic pH of 6.0 to 6.5. Demands consistent moisture, especially during fruit production. Thrives in full sun.

whatToFeed: Rich organic compost top-dress paired with an organic high-potassium berry fertilizer in early spring.
pruningTips: As an everbearing type, you can cut all canes completely down to the ground in late winter for a massive single late-summer crop, or leave strong canes to produce a small July harvest followed by a giant autumn flush.
flavorProfile: Bright, tart, and deeply aromatic classic red raspberry sweetness.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant bare-root canes in early spring (mid-to-late April) or early autumn.
whenToHarvest: Harvest continuously in July, and again from late August until the first hard winter frost. Berries are ready when they slip effortlessly off the central white core.
usesForLargeHarvests: Mashing into cocktail simple syrups, freezing in single layers, baking into fruit tarts, crafting fresh coulis.
hardinessNote: Extremely tough, cold-hardy perennial bramble. Will spread over time via underground root suckers.
plantingInstructions: Dig a trench 1 foot deep and wide. Space individual canes 2-3 feet apart in rows. Gently cover roots with loose soil, keeping the crown 1 inch below the soil surface line. Water deeply and apply mulch to retain cane hydration.
growthRequirements: Thrives in deep, loose, highly fertile loam with top-tier drainage capability and a pH of 5.6 to 6.2. Sensitive roots will suffer in heavy, pooling standing water. Prefers broad full sun but accepts light partial afternoon shade.

whatToFeed: Balanced organic berry food or well-rotted manure applied in early spring as new shoots emerge.
pruningTips: In summer, tip-prune new green canes when they reach 4 feet tall to force lateral branching. In winter, prune away the dead wood of old canes that finished fruiting, leaving new smooth canes for next season.
flavorProfile: Sweet, bold, and richly complex with a high juice concentration and zero bitter edge.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant container starts or bare-roots in mid-to-late April once the soil can be cultivated easily.
whenToHarvest: Harvest in mid-to-late summer when the glossy berries turn a deep, dull black color and detach with zero pulling resistance.
usesForLargeHarvests: Baking into crumbles or tarts, infusing into spirits or simple syrups, canning into preserves, freezing whole.
hardinessNote: Perennial thornless variety; smooth canes make harvesting simple and scratch-free without sacrificing heavy yields.
plantingInstructions: Dig holes slightly deeper and wider than the container housing. Space multiple plants 4-5 feet apart down a line, setting a simple wire support system to help tie up long heavy summer branches. Set root crowns flush with the surrounding soil.
growthRequirements: Demands deeply cultivated, moisture-retentive, yet fast-draining clay-loam soils with a preferred pH of 6.0 to 6.8. Requires uniform irrigation while fruit is swelling. Demands complete full sun exposure to sweeten the berries.
